speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. My name is Myra Slotnick. I'm interviewing Harold Blitzer. It's September 5th, , in Las Vegas, Nevada. Harold, tell me under what circumstances you came to Las Vegas and what you've been doing for the last several years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I came to Las Vegas sometime in '68, 1968. I-- it was two of us. I had a partner. His name was-- what the hell was his name? I forgot. [LAUGHS] I forget things. Custer, his name was, Don Custer. I think C-U-S-T-E-R. And he always told me he was German. I found out later that he was a Polack [LAUGHS] who joined the German army. Probably was a Nazi. And [LAUGHS] so him and I started a business here. We were-- I had a deal cooking from-- from a-- a Florida. I came out of Florida from a Florida land company. And we needed some time. The OK from the owner, and to come in here to make a-- to exempt us from, at that time, a real estate license. Things like that of the owner, because we were an employee of a corporation.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, this guy never showed up. And I met Foxy, Abe Fox. And Abe Fox, I used to go have breakfast at a place, you know. And so Abe Fox started talking to me. He said, what are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, we were selling for somebody else in-- in Hawaiian land, you know. The guy says to me, hey, I got all this land in Pahrump. He says, come and take a ride with me one Sunday. We went out there. Showed me the land. I says, well, hey, that's great. I said this is Las Vegas. This is a suburb of Las Vegas. And he made a deal with us. So-- so we set up a corporation. And it was called American International Development. And if you abbreviate it, that's how we used to-- [LAUGHS] it was called the AID Corporation. That was long before. We used to answer the phone-- AID Corporation. Thank god we didn't last that long till today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started selling. And like any type of company in the land business, we worked on deposits and cash flow. We-- we reached-- we were selling so much. And we always had the cash flow problem, because you didn't get paid in full. You got like 10% down, or many times, you only got 5% down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And payments, we had a split with Foxy. He got half of it. So we always were in trouble, always financially in trouble. So-- but we put up about, the first year, we had-- we showed on a statement about $400,000 profit before taxes. Because at that time, the accounting principle, it was different than it was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were dealing with a man in Phoenix who was like a [INAUDIBLE]. So he advanced us some money. And then he says, I have a corporation. I was interested in buying you off, you know, with management contract and so forth. So it was a company called Safeguard Industry out of Philadelphia. So he brought them in, and they wanted that profit picture, which I was not aware at that time. But we sold the company. And I don't want to go into that kind of details because it was a bad deal, because we had to go to court and things like that. So in '75, when-- when my contract was up with Safeguard Industry, I started going into the casino promotion business. And I had different casinos on a contract, including Bob Stupak, and the Boyd Group, and Foxy's, and whatever, you know, like the Silver City, and the Major Riddle's places, and Stupak, Vegas World, and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always-- I always worked as an independent contractor at that time. And did very, fairly very well, but also produced. You know, it wasn't something I didn't do. And my interest was always art at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I found out that the Desert Inn had a gallery for sale-- one of the owners wanted to get out-- I bought it. And-- and I started also handling art. And of course, the mistake was it was the Desert Inn. The art store was way back. Nobody could find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so, I had it for a number of years, and then I sold it to a LA outfit, who then stayed with it for about six months and then closed it up anyways. So I got my money out. And then I stayed in the promotion business. Here, I've been here since 1968, '68.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm looking here on 28 years, and I've been in the same house almost from the beginning, you know. And of course, I remodeled it a couple of times. You know, I never liked this place. But now, it's becoming--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] --it's becoming such a great place. And I'm back in the promotion business. And-- and that's about my story here. You know, I used to handle the promotion for-- wasn't that great of a promotion for the Desert Inn, because we've got some crazy people out there. And I am looking to get into another thing. And I'm negotiating with some different casino people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what kind of hobbies do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I play golf, and I play racquetball. And one time, I used to collect stamps, sort of a special-- not the individual stamps, just blocks, of-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Commemorative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Commemorative issues, and I've still got a bunch of them. And-- and my hobbies, I like girls. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're wild. You-- your education was limited.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very limited. I-- I left school when I was 12 years old. I-- my education comes out of books. I love books. I read books like somebody-- I devour books, you know. And-- and I like to read books while the TV is on. I'm not alone. I don't look at the TV, but somebody is speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how many languages do you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I spoke at one time, perfect Danish. But I've been out of practice of it. I speak German, of course, and Yiddish. Yiddish I picked up in a camp. At one time, I spoke pretty good Czechoslovakian. But these are the types of language that you don't practice. If you don't practice those things, and Danish specially. I recently, a couple of years ago, I joined a Danish club here. I found out I could hardly-- it took me a while just to understand what was going on. And so in other words, theoretically, I'll always speak German and-- and a little bit English. And I don't know if Yiddish is a language. I think it's a dying language. The young people don't learn any Yiddish anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you to begin to speak about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] I tell you what. What happened was I received an invitation to go to Israel for our first reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, that's got to be like 12, 13 years ago, I think. I-- I-- and I didn't go. I wanted to go, and I didn't want to go, right. Then they had the reunion in Washington. I says, I got to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was under Reagan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that had to be like 12, 12 years now-- 10, 12 years ago, right? '80-- was it like '88 or something like that? Yeah. So before I went to Washington, I requested a-- some kind of official letter from the B'nai B'rith. And-- but all the meetings, nothing happened. I went to Governor Bryan. Gave me a beautiful proclamation, which is now an exhibit, at the Holocaust Museum. I had it-- I framed it up. I added on a frame. And I presented it to Benjamin Meed, one of the head of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were on national television when I made the presentation. And I invited our senator. What was his name, our ambassador, to the Bahamas? [LAUGHS] He never showed up. But what the heck was his name, right? He says he's too busy. I says, yeah, that's probably why you never got re-elected. But I invited him for the presentation. And he never-- he said he's too busy to attend to it, right. So I still have some pictures of it. I gave the governor-- and we talk about it occasionally. And I told him that-- he's now in the Senate. I told him you ought to go and take a look at your proclamation every time and then. They now have it in the study room or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that's when I started talking. That's all of sudden, all of us started talking. All of a sudden, all of the survivors are now together, right. There was a couple thousand people there, right. And all of a sudden, we talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? Where weren't you? A friend of mine from New York called me. Says I got a friend of mine. His name is so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: He also was in a camp. So it turned out we were in the same camp together. He was-- he came out of Auschwitz because they were routing it back. So I never saw the guy because, [LAUGHS] you know, 15,000 people, or 12,000, you don't see everybody. But it turned out we were in the same camp together. And we've been friends ever since. Matter of fact, he has the same disease Steve Wynn has got, tunnel vision. And there-- I met him here again a couple of years ago when they have a convention over at the Mirage, you know. And they go-- yeah, they just took away his license and things like that, you know, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what do you do with your information? How do you help the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I-- I go out on the request which come into the Jewish Federation. And whenever they have a school they want me to go to, and now the schools call me direct, [LAUGHS] you know. They don't-- because I've been at the same school so many times, new students in. And-- and I tell them what I-- what I sort of experienced. I talk a great deal about Denmark, because Denmark was the only country who helped the Jews, who really made an effort. Even after they got arrested, they protected them in a sense. But they couldn't protect them against hunger. They couldn't protect them against diseases. But we still had 70 some people died, you know. But that's out of 470. That's also a good percentage. But these people were not killed, per se. They were starved to death. They were sick and something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you only were starved to death. This is like it's-- like it's nothing, you know. You look at it. Only 77 people got killed. [LAUGHS] One is too many.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so this is when we started changing. A lot of us started changing. All of a sudden, we started realizing we are getting older. If we want to tell our story, we better tell it now. And don't forget then that all of these denialists came up, all these people on television. I remember one time I was in California, switching-- fooling around with the cable. All of a sudden, I hear a guy in a Nazi uniform telling about, oh, there was never any Holocaust. That was just a propaganda by Israel to get money out of Germany and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to see that son of a gun, because I can [LAUGHS] hit him a couple of times over the head. Now, I can hit back. And so we experienced the same thing, like, hey, let's-- let's tell that story. Let's-- let enough people know because-- see, the Holocaust has never really stopped, you know. You have Holocaust in Africa. You kill each other in Northern Ireland, two civilized countries, you know. You say how can the Germans do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans did it, and the Irish did it. And the Russians doing it. And the Poles are doing it. And the Africans are doing it to each other. The Arabs are killing each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so where's it gonna to end, unless we educate the young people? They are the ones who are going to. It's too late for us. We can't change the world. But these are the kids who are going to change the world one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're going to find out what it is to be, to live in peace, you know. Hopefully, they're going-- they'll learn something, you know. I usually close, when I talk to these kids, I usually close with the following. I like them to-- I said, look. I says, I'm going to ask you a favor. And I'm actually going to give you kids a job. I don't call them kids. I call them young people. I say, I want to give you a job. I want to say to you-- look, if somebody comes along and says, 'there was no Holocaust,' you're going to say, 'I have a friend. His name is Harold.' And I hope I am your friend. And he told me that he experienced firsthand there was a Holocaust. I don't believe you. You're wrong. And I believe Harold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what I want from all of you. Is that OK? And then-- and I get them involved a little bit. And I-- I'll tell you something. I get a lot of letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got hundreds of letters from these kids, you know. And I thought it was like an exception. Then I found out they have to do it as part of their English. [LAUGHS] I have a dear friend of mine. He's-- he's the-- he's the one in charge of the-- the high school, you know. And I told him about it. I said, you know, I talk to 3,000 of your kids. I never got a single letter. And I get all these letters. So he told me [LAUGHS] that this is their job. But on the other hand, it's enjoyable to get to read these letters. I read every one of them. And I try to answer them. I answered them in a group, and I send one to the teacher. Please post it if it's OK and things like that. And I'll tell you one thing. The kids are fantastic. And it seems like the older the kids are, the worst of an audience they become. It's the young kids I-- I-- I get so much. Once they go into high school-- and I did some-- I worked with Professor [? Mattson ?] over at the university. I went to one. He asked me to come to one of his classes. Not a single person asked a question. And boy, do I get questions, [LAUGHS] strange questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has the Holocaust affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in a sense, it has affected me. And I'm using this because this is one of the questions people ask me. And it's-- it's-- and I can only answer it as follows. I say, look, he says-- I never had a Sunday dinner with my family. Now, it's not just a Sunday dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had no place I belonged to. You follow me? I remember when I was in the service, and we had a three day pass. And the kids used to roll up their dirty laundry and take it home to mama. And Monday morning, they were-- they were clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to stay in camp and do my own laundry. And I used to-- I used to dislike people. They have a family. And-- and this is, you know, when I say I had-- I-- it's not just the dinner. It's the all around. From the day I was 13 years old I had nobody to go home to. When something happens in your life, you-- when you're young, you run across something, you want to go home to Mother, just to-- to stay there a couple of days, just to-- you know, just to talk things over and recharge yourself. I-- I would say that-- that's probably-- that's my-- my biggest problem. I've been-- I've always been a loner. And I became, as you go on, you become lonelier and lonelier. That's why I read with the TV on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you never got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] For many years, I didn't realize why I never got married. Then I started-- I do a lot of research on things, you know. I like-- I like to know what makes everything tick, you know. And I'm not an expert on everything. And I sort of started realizing as I got to know many of the survivors, I found out the majority married people in camp, married people they were with in camp. And I've met a lot of women in my life. And-- and I mean, I'm not a Casanova. Maybe I was. But I-- I-- I got the personality I can make friends easy. I-- I do make friends, because I know I talk a little bit. And I get along. I get along with a lot of people. And I could never get myself to marry somebody who was not on my level with the experience. Because I figured sooner or later, it's not going to work out. And it seems like most of the people I know have married people they were with one way or the other in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I came out of camp before. That was also one of my big disappointment. I didn't stay there till the end. I figured it was over. And in one way, I was glad, because you never knew what they were going to do, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you wanted to stay there to see those bastards get hung, you know. They hung the commandant. The Czechs came in. And the Russians hung him immediately. You know, like nailed him right there, you know. By the way, the camp was not liberized till about the 12th of May. That's like five days after there was no-- no war. The war was over, but this was the last of the camp the Russians got into.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in one way, I was glad, and in the other way, I was not glad because I would have loved to be there taking little-- you-- you build up certain revenge, you know. You become very hateful. I-- I remember one thing. When I came to the United States with my broken English, I went to Times Square. That's where the recruiting office was for the military. I want to join the army, I said. And the guy says, yeah? And what do you want to do? I said I want to go to Germany. I want you to get me to Germany. What are you going to do in Germany? I said, I want to kill Germans. He says, kid, the war is over. Go on home. And by the way, this Henry is a very interesting story. He got here in June of 1946. And he was less than two months [LAUGHS] in the United States when he was drafted. At that time, the draft was still in order. Now, guess where they're sending him? They're sending him to Berlin, right? Now, he becomes a-- a-- he becomes part of the OSS. And he speaks German and things like that. And-- [LAUGHS] and after two years, he takes a discharge and stays as a civilian employed with the-- with the CIA or whatever they call it, you know. And he probably, [LAUGHS] I think, he told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him one time in Berlin, you know. I saw him, and we talked about it. He says, I haven't been back in the States. And he had nobody either. He had no family, no nothing, you know. And so he hardly ever went on leave back to the United States. And he stayed there like 20 some years with the military. Then I lost touch with him, you know. And I tried to find him over in the computer and the-- and at our first reunion. And I couldn't find him.

speaker: interviewer
text: One last question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Honestly, no. I'm not miserable, but I'm not happy. I'm-- I-- I don't know. I'm-- I consider myself very lonely, very lonely. I have friends, but [SIGHS] I don't know. I-- I don't really think I have friends I could say, look, this is what-- I don't have a family. I go every year to see my cousin in-- in South America. And I feel very comfortable there. His wife makes me very comfortable. I look forward to it, you know. And I really-- I spend a month down there in South America. And-- and it's-- for one thing or the other, I don't like South America per se, but I like the-- like the-- like the families I got. So you make your own answer up. I don't like-- I-- I really don't know how to answer that am I happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I am. I may-- you know, I'm not unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm not happy. You're very welcome. [LAUGHTER] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather. He is from [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a little village outside of Krakow. And my father was born in [NON-ENGLISH] also. So he used to visit us occasionally for the holidays, like Passover. And when he was there, we really had a-- a regular Passover. We always had a Passover service no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think this had to be taken some time, I would say, right before the Second World War or right after, something like that. Matter of fact, there was a-- I never paid attention. There was actually, on the back of the picture, there was a year, you know. But I had it-- I framed it up, and I got it only a couple of years ago in South America. My cousin brought it-- brought it to me. They had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house my father was born. There were seven brothers and two girls, total of nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest sister went to Israel. The youngest brother, [NON-ENGLISH]-- when-- [? Ida ?] was the sister in Israel. Oh, and [NON-ENGLISH] was the youngest of the brothers. He went to Israel in 1932, and so did [? Ida. ?] And the other sister went to South America in 1938 with-- with my uncle and his two-- his two sons. And she had two-- two sons also. Oh, no, she had two sons and-- and one girl, the older sister. And of which both the-- the-- the daughter [INAUDIBLE] is alive. And-- and-- and the youngest boy of her was Bernard. The oldest brother died a couple of years ago in South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was this house at?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in [NON-ENGLISH] Poland, a little city outside Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Got a little bit of a wedding picture here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us where it was taken, you think [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was their wedding pictures, if I'm not mistaken. And I don't know. I know they were married in Krakow. And oh, thank you. And could be-- could be probably taken 1918, '20, or maybe even in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isn't there any-- wait a minute. Isn't there anything on the back of these pictures? Oops. Let me get my glasses here so I know what I'm doing. I got them right here. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now this one hasn't got a date.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my father, my mother, of course, and my sister. I would say my sister's about four or five years old, maybe even before I was born. [LAUGHS] Could be, if she was four years old there. Yeah, and that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Berlin, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know who this naked person is. Now, it is actually me. I think I'm about two or three months old, I guess. How would you-- older?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you're at least a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a gorgeous baby. You gotta say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice tush. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister on her first day of school. This is sort of a German-- German thing, like this-- this thing was sort of filled with candy. And you got that on your first day of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see the thing on the back? That's-- that's how you went to school in the old days. And it says right here on the blackboard, 'my first day in school'-- 'my first school year.' My sister, Lilli. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister and myself. Looks to me like three or four years old, I guess, right? Yeah, because that means my sister has to be like eight. She was four years older than me. And this was taken somewhere. I know it was taken in Berlin. It looks like in our yard, because there's a water thing there, a water spout-- water spout. Look at her hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was our living room, or dining room. And this was at our apartment, which was above the store we had at the Chestnut Street. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the Chestnut Street-- 87, come to think of it. God, what a memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that [INAUDIBLE] wall behind. That was a typical-- my mother used to make tea in it. And these were our best dishes, of course. [LAUGHS] And it was-- it had to be taken sometime in the beginning of '30, right? 1932 or something like that, yeah. OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my sister, Lilli. I would say she was probably 16, 17 years old. I think it's probably the last picture she ever took. And I think it was soon after-- soon after that I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This had to be taken in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my cousin. I'm sorry-- my uncle and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And uncle was called David. His wife was called [? Berta. ?] [INAUDIBLE], And this-- this is Harry, the oldest brother. And this is Martin, that's the guy I always visit. Harry is now in Israel. This-- this is the oldest-- [SIGHS] the oldest sister. It's not-- it's not the whole family there. There's somebody must have taken the picture. That was on their way to Paris on their way to South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That had to be 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany. Actually, he was deported to Poland, but he had permission to come back into Germany to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my father and myself in 1957 in Paris. Where-- this was the first time I've seen my-- my father after the liberation. And the reason is very simple. [LAUGHS] I didn't make any money enough to go to Palestine. And he didn't have enough money to go to United States, so. Plus, it was not easy to get travel in those years any where.
